notes: The Alaska Center published a whitepaper on data center impacts and has been monitoring data center development statewide. In Wrangell specifically, opposition came from individual community members and the public advisory board rather than a formal local group. Board member Jillian Privett called for more community input. A Cordova resident testified that Greensparc's data center there 'has been underwhelming and underperforming.' The planning commission narrowly recommended approval of the lease.

No local named opposition group found specific to Pike County. The moratorium was one of four passed across Georgia counties in September 2025 (Pike, Lamar, Troup, Clayton). The Sierra Club Georgia Chapter has been actively advocating for data center regulation statewide, including supporting HB 528 (transparency), SB 34 (grid upgrade costs), and HB 559 (ending tax breaks). Pike County Commissioner James Jenkins supported the moratorium to better understand how to accommodate data centers.

No local named opposition group found specific to Lamar County. Amazon purchased 985 acres in Lamar County for $270M. Commission Chairman Ryran Traylor was more welcoming than neighboring counties, stating they 'look forward to engaging in productive discussions with Amazon.' The moratorium was part of the same wave as Pike, Troup, and Clayton counties. The Sierra Club Georgia Chapter has been the primary statewide advocacy organization opposing data center expansion.

No named opposition group found. The Change.org petition 'Stop the construction of a data center in Plainfield, Indiana' has 200 signatures (goal: 500). Over a dozen residents opposed the RadiusDC project at public comment. The Plainfield Plan Commission unanimously approved the primary plat petition despite opposition. Residents cited concerns about electricity costs, water usage, and limited economic return.

No named opposition group found. The moratorium passed unanimously without debate or public comments. This was a proactive moratorium driven by supervisors' concerns about water pollution, power needs, and siting, rather than organized grassroots opposition.

No formal named opposition group, but significant grassroots organizing by residents Andy and Carrie Hoefer, who designed and sent 2,000+ postcards and knocked on hundreds of doors to rally neighbors. Emails to council ran 20-to-1 against the $2.5B Active Infrastructure project. The council voted 7-0 to reject the proposal. Former U.S. Sen. Kyrsten Sinema lobbied in favor but was rebuffed. Eric Runnestrand was another prominent resident opponent.

No formal named opposition group found. Nearly 20 public speakers opposed bringing the data center to the community, with every speaker opposing it and receiving applause. Diana Deitz, a Fayette County resident, has been fighting data centers for three years since QTS first proposed a campus in Fayetteville. The Planning & Zoning Commission denied Corvus Investment Group's rezoning request, recommending Light Industrial zoning instead of Business Park. The commission's decision is advisory and can be appealed to City Council.

No formal named opposition group, but Gregory P. Guido Jr. of Harlem filed two judicial review petitions on Feb. 25 challenging the Board of Commissioners' approval of the White Oak Tech Park (Trammell Crow/CBRE) and Pumpkin Center data center rezonings. Guido stated he was 'trying to be the voice of the people.' Both petitions allege procedural defects, incomplete analysis, and conflicts with the county's comprehensive plan. The White Oak project was approved unanimously with noise conditions; Pumpkin Center was approved 4-1.

No formal named opposition group, but organized neighborhood opposition included 29 physicians along with engineers, attorneys, and PR professionals who came together to create 'a thoughtful and professional opposition.' Neighbors complained CyrusOne's existing data center produced noise described as 'like there was a helicopter on our roof.' The city approved a six-month moratorium and reached a legal agreement with CyrusOne on noise mitigation. New performance standards for noise, water-use reporting, and energy plans are being drafted.

No local named opposition group found specific to Cobb County. Commissioners proposed a 180-day moratorium on data center applications for unincorporated areas. The Cobb County-Marietta Water Authority warned some facilities can consume over 1 million gallons of water per day. A draft resolution listed potential harms including noise pollution, air quality impacts, 'undesirable aesthetics,' and strain on power and water utilities. The Sierra Club Georgia Chapter has been the primary statewide advocacy organization.

No named opposition group found. Franklin County Commissioners voted unanimously for a year-long moratorium on data center applications, effective until February 24, 2027. The moratorium allows the county to learn more about infrastructure requirements and develop new zoning codes. Residents voiced opposition citing concerns about water use, electricity costs, noise, heat, and quality-of-life issues. The Planning Commission continues work on a new zoning code.

Opposition was primarily driven by anti-solar activism that expanded to include data centers. Bobby Rauen created the Change.org petition (1,743 signatures) and collected ~2,200 physical signatures calling for a moratorium. Hundreds packed a town hall meeting. The moratorium on solar farms, battery storage, and data centers was approved unanimously on Feb. 25, lasting one year. The petition primarily focused on solar but the moratorium covers data centers too.

Youth Action Fund (board chair Anamikda Naidu) expressed support for HB 1007, which would restrict hyperscale data center siting. Gov. DeSantis pushed for AI guardrails. The House passed HB 1007 (24-1) to prohibit data centers within 5 miles of schools/residential property unless unanimously approved by local government, and ban NDAs with developers. The Senate unanimously approved companion bill SB 484. Industry group NetChoice opposed the bill, arguing it would effectively ban large data centers in Florida.

No named opposition group found. This is federal legislation: the GRID Act (Guaranteeing Rate Insulation from Data Centers Act) introduced by Sen. Richard Blumenthal (D-CT) and Sen. Josh Hawley (R-MO). The bill requires new data centers over 20 MW to find power sources outside the grid and report estimated utility usage. Violations carry $1M/day civil fines. The bill has not yet gained traction in Congress. At the state level, Connecticut's Energy and Technology Committee held hearings on revoking data center tax incentives (SB 245).

No named opposition group found, but strong community engagement shaped the ordinance. Over 100 residents attended public hearings in Palo. Dorothy Landt of Palo called for a complete moratorium. Laura Krouse raised concerns about the adequacy of water study requirements. The Board of Supervisors unanimously approved one of the nation's most comprehensive data center zoning ordinances, requiring water studies, 1,000-foot setbacks from residential property, noise/light limits, and community betterment fund contributions. Google subsequently pulled out of building in unincorporated Linn County, pivoting to seek annexation into the city of Palo.

The Facebook group 'No Data Centers, Fulton County' was created by Akron resident James Zimmerman and grew to 2,600+ members in a county of ~20,000 people. The group emerged in response to plans for a potential 500MW data center by the Decennial Group. The Area Plan Commission voted 6-1 to approve a moratorium after a standing-room-only hearing, and commissioners later voted 2-1 for a one-year moratorium on data center construction.

No named opposition group found. Weld County is proactively drafting data center regulations (keeping projects in industrial/agricultural zones with site plan or special review). Global AI purchased 438 acres near Windsor for $15.6M for a facility that could scale from 18-24MW to 1GW. Residents raised concerns about water usage, noise, and infrastructure. The county is allowing projects to proceed while setting new rules, in contrast to Denver's moratorium approach.

A Change.org petition 'Stop data center in Muscogee County' has 1,814 signatures. The proposed $5.18B Habitat Partners data center campus would be on ~900 acres in northeast Muscogee County. Resident Troy Keller advocated for a 180-day moratorium, citing noise, light, and paving concerns. The Columbus City Council discussed the project with residents voicing concerns about neighborhood character, environmental conditions, traffic, and infrastructure capacity.

No named opposition group found, but strong grassroots opposition drove the first local data center moratorium in Ohio (9 months, September 2025). Trustee Wezlynn Davis noted standing-room-only meetings at any mention of data centers. Residents complained about constant 'industrial buzz' noise from existing Amazon data centers. Fire Chief Douglas Stewart cited two construction worker fatalities and a $50M fire at Amazon sites. Amazon's two data centers have 10-year tax abatements approved by Union County. Four projects received zoning permits in December 2025 despite the moratorium.

No formal named opposition group, but former Republican state lawmaker Gene Krebs helped lead opposition. The planning commission denied the rezoning 11-0, and the rural zoning commission denied it 5-0. Overcrowded public hearings saw residents bringing garden chairs for seating. Developer Patrick Scanlon withdrew the rezoning application on September 29 as residents organized a referendum campaign. Krebs said he'd never seen citizens so 'universally upset and perturbed' about an issue in the county.

Resident Cassia Core organized a Change.org petition that has grown to 3,866 signatures (goal: 5,000). The petition opposes converting the IX Center (which hosted the Cleveland Auto Show, Christmas Connection, and other events serving 30,000 vendors annually) into an AI data center. The proposed facility would provide fewer than 300 permanent jobs. The city of Cleveland has stated it has no interest in having the property used as a data center.

No named opposition group found. An estimated 150 residents packed the Wood County Planning Commission meeting demanding answers about the Meta data center and requesting a moratorium. Mark Patton, who lives a mile from the site, said residents came out of 'anger and fear.' NDAs kept Meta's name secret even after construction began. The 350 MW Apollo gas plant was approved by the Ohio Power Siting Board with no public hearings, further enraging residents. Ohio law does not give counties authority to issue moratoriums for specific land uses, limiting the planning commission's options.

No specific named opposition group found for Sunbury. Dozens of residents packed public meetings opposing Amazon's proposed $2B data center that would rezone 330+ acres for industrial use. Meetings reached capacity with overflow rooms needed. Residents raised concerns about health, environmental impacts, water quality, and diesel generator emissions. The public hearing was postponed due to an error with parcel numbers and rescheduled for March 23, 2026.

No named opposition group found. Blanchester Village Council unanimously approved a preemptive moratorium (Ordinance 2026.007) on Feb. 12, 2026, following data center proposals in nearby Wilmington and Mount Orab. Officials are clarifying whether land near the corporation limit is covered or if annexation would be needed to prevent data center development. Part of a broader wave of ~18 Ohio municipalities considering or enacting data center moratoriums.

Amanda Doughty, a lifelong Peninsula resident, created the Change.org petition which has grown to 4,291 signatures (goal: 5,000). The petition went from 100 signatures within 24 hours to over 3,500 within a week. A separate petition on thepetitionsite.com titled 'No Data Centers in Newport News' also exists. Vice Mayor Curtis Bethany later clarified there are 'no conversations, plans, or negotiations underway to bring a data center to Newport News,' though the city has been buying land near Fort Eustis.
